sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=57, col=6).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 37, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Clicking the purple object reset the level - it disappeared and greens reappeared. The purple object was a reset/undo button. Now we have greens again to consume.
- Goal model: Restart the green consumption pattern from the current state.
- Plan: Check current green positions and resume the consumption sequence.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 9 of about 81 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now we have 5 greens again at:
- (16, 14), (16, 20), (16, 32), (16, 44) in the top grid
- (52, 44) in the bottom grid
This is the initial state. The pattern I discovered earlier was:
1. Click between two adjacent greens → selects one with G border, creates g marks between next two greens
2. Click on g marks → consumes selected green, creates new green at click position
The greens are spaced at intervals: 14, 20, 32, 44 in the top row.
- Between 14 and 20: midpoint ~17
- Between 20 and 32: midpoint ~26
- Between 32 and 44: midpoint ~38
From earlier experience:
- Click at (16, 17) between (16, 14) and (16, 20) → selected (16, 14), g marks at (16, 26)
- Click at (16, 26) → consumed (16, 14), new green at (16, 26)
- Click at (16, 29) between (16, 26) and (16, 32) → selected (16, 26), g marks at (16, 38)
- Click at (16, 38) → consumed (16, 26), new green at (16, 38)
- Click at (16, 42) between (16, 38) and (16, 44) → selected (16, 44), g marks at (16, 32)
- Click at (16, 32) → consumed ALL remaining greens
So the sequence to consume all greens is:
1. Click (16, 17) → select (16, 14), g marks at (16, 26)
2. Click (16, 26) → consume (16, 14), new green at (16, 26)
3. Click (16, 29) → select (16, 26), g marks at (16, 38)
4. Click (16, 38) → consume (16, 26), new green at (16, 38)
5. Click (16, 42) → select (16, 44), g marks at (16, 32)
6. Click (16, 32) → consume all remaining greens
I can execute this entire sequence in one batched action to clear all the greens efficiently.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Reset to initial state with 5 greens at (16, 14), (16, 20), (16, 32), (16, 44), (52, 44). The consumption pattern is now well understood.
Goal model: Execute the full consumption sequence to clear all greens.
Plan: Batch the 6-step sequence: click between greens to select, click g marks to consume, repeat until all consumed.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Execute the full consumption sequence in one batch
# Step 1: Click (16, 17) between (16,14) and (16,20) -> selects (16,14), g marks at (16,26)
# Step 2: Click (16, 26) -> consumes (16,14), new green at (16,26)
# Step 3: Click (16, 29) between (16,26) and (16,32) -> selects (16,26), g marks at (16,38)
# Step 4: Click (16, 38) -> consumes (16,26), new green at (16,38)
# Step 5: Click (16, 42) between (16,38) and (16,44) -> selects (16,44), g marks at (16,32)
# Step 6: Click (16, 32) -> consumes all remaining greens

result = action([
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 16, 'col': 17},
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 16, 'col': 26},
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 16, 'col': 29},
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 16, 'col': 38},
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 16, 'col': 42},
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 16, 'col': 32}
])

# Check final state
seg = current_frame.segmentation
n_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
g_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] in ('G', 'g')]
print(f"Remaining N nodes: {len(n_nodes)}")
print(f"G/g nodes: {len(g_nodes)}")
for n in g_nodes:
    center = (sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])//len(n['boundary']), sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])//len(n['boundary']))
    print(f"  {n['color']}: center={center}")

print(f"Done: {last_action_result.get('done', False)}")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Remaining N nodes: 0
G/g nodes: 2
  g: center=(16, 32)
  g: center=(52, 44)
Done: False
Level completed: False
